Title: The plant trans-Golgi network component ECHIDNA regulates defense, cell death, and endoplasmic reticulum stress

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling against biotrophic pathogens; activation of innate immunity and hypersensitive response
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of ECH causes salicylic acid hyperaccumulation, constitutive defense activation, PMR4-dependent callose accumulation, spontaneous cell death, and chronic ER stress; enhances postinvasion resistance to powdery mildew but compromises penetration resistance
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: loss-of-function mutant analysis; genetic complementation with pECH:ECH-YFP; double-mutant analysis with sid2-1, NahG, jar1-1, ein2-1, and pmr4-1; hormone quantification by UPLC/ESI-MS/MS; RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; confocal microscopy; pathogen assays
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: powdery mildew infection by Erysiphe cichoracearum; nonadapted Blumeria graminis f. sp. hordei infection; tunicamycin treatment; dithiothreitol treatment
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ECH is required for proper secretion of plasma membrane and apoplastic defense cargoes. 2. Loss of ECH causes constitutive salicylic acid hyperaccumulation through the ICS1 pathway and activates SA-dependent immunity. 3. ECH loss also triggers SA-independent spontaneous cell death, PMR4-dependent callose deposition, and chronic ER stress.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ECH is not a biosynthetic enzyme or transcriptional regulator; it acts as a trafficking component linking secretion defects to altered defense metabolite status.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating ECH is not an obvious breeding target because loss of function causes severe dwarfism, spontaneous cell death, and broad cellular stress despite enhanced resistance to powdery mildew.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe dwarf phenotype; compromised penetration resistance; increased susceptibility to necrotrophic pathogen Sclerotinia sclerotiorum; spontaneous cell death; chlorosis under stress
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: pECH (native, complementation); transgenes in studied backgrounds include 35S and pUBQ reporters

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: leaf epidermal cells and cell walls
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cell wall-associated defense polymer deposited during stress and pathogen responses
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane; intracellular aggregates in ech mutant
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for constitutive callose accumulation induced by ECH disruption; loss of PMR4 abolishes the callose aggregates in ech
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Moderately upregulated in ech leaves relative to wild type
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: double-mutant analysis with ech pmr4-1; aniline blue staining; RT-qPCR; GFP-PMR4 localization and colocalization microscopy
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PMR4 is essential for the constitutive callose deposition observed in ech. 2. PMR4 transcript is modestly increased in ech. 3. In ech, PMR4 relocalizes into intracellular bodies that colocalize with callose aggregates, suggesting secretion-dependent control of PMR4 activity.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study supports post-transcriptional/translocation-based regulation of PMR4 activity rather than simple transcriptional control.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: PMR4 may be useful for engineering callose-based defense, but ectopic activation could exacerbate growth defects or cell death depending on trafficking status.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: pmr4-1 in ech background accelerates dwarfism/chlorosis and cell death while removing callose aggregates

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts chorismate to isochorismate in the plastid, supplying the major precursor route for salicylic acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies immediate precursor route for target product
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling against biotrophic pathogens; hypersensitive response
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutively upregulated in ech under pathogen-free conditions; induced by Ec infection in wild type
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ICS1-dependent pathway mediates SA hyperaccumulation in ech and is largely responsible for enhanced postinvasion resistance to powdery mildew
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: genetic analysis using sid2-1/ICS1-defective double mutant; RNA-seq; SA quantification; pathogen resistance assays
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: powdery mildew infection by Erysiphe cichoracearum
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as key control step in this study for SA hyperaccumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Genes in the ICS1-mediated SA biosynthesis pathway are coordinately upregulated in ech. 2. SA hyperaccumulation in ech depends genetically on SID2/ICS1. 3. Loss of ICS1 function largely suppresses enhanced powdery mildew resistance and HR-associated responses in ech.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper contrasts ICS1 pathway activation with lack of activation of the PAL-mediated SA pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ICS1 is a strong target for tuning SA-mediated disease resistance, but activation may incur defense-growth tradeoffs.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Elevated SA signaling is associated with dwarfism and defense activation in the ech background
